coredata.dc:title: MOF@N–doped carbon dots–based dispersive solid phase extraction combined dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction of several aflatoxins from raw cow’s milk samples prior to HPLC–FLD
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Khalilipour, Ghadir
Karamibonari, Amir Reza
Movassaghghazani, Mohammadhosein
Shayegh, Jalal
Afshar Mogaddam, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: A dispersive solid-phase extraction method based on metal–organic framework and nitrogen–doped carbon dot nanocomposite combined with magnetic ionic liquid-based dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction was established for the extraction of aflatoxins from milk samples. For this purpose, the proteins in the sample were precipitated using a zinc sulfate solution. After vortexing (for 5 min) and centrifuging, 35 mg of the sorbent were added into the aqueous phase obtained from previous step, and the mixture was vortexed for five minutes. In the following, the sorbent particles were separated and eluted with methanol in the presence of vortex agitation (4 min). After centrifuging, the eluent phase was mixed with magnetic ionic liquid (1- octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrachloroferrate) and rapidly injected into deionized water to perform the dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction procedure. Finally, the magnetic solvent was separated in the presence of a magnet and injected into a high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence detector after diluting with the mobile phase. Referring to the results, the limits of detection and quantification of the studied aflatoxins were in the ranges of 0.69–1.09 and 2.31–3.63 ng/L, respectively. Extraction recoveries were in the range of 61 to 73 %. At the end, the suggested approach was employed in the analysis of the aflatoxins in the raw cow’s milk samples collected from different regions of East Azarbaijan and AFM1 was found in several samples (34 out of 60 samples). Also, AFB1 was found in three samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxin
Dispersive solid phase extraction
Metal organic framework
Carbon dot
Milk
High performance liquid chromatography

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Study zinc sulfate solution (a) concentration and (b) volume. Conditions: (a): sample, 5 mL spiked blank raw cow's milk spiked with 500 ng L − 1 of each AF; zinc sulfate solution volume, 1 mL; vortexing time, 5 min; centrifuging time (speed), 5 min (5000 rpm); sorbent amount, 30 mg; adsorption time, 5 min; centrifuging time (speed), 5 min (5000 rpm); eluent type (volume), ACN (1.0 mL); desorption time, 5 min; extractant type (volume), [C8MIM] [FeCl4] MIL (60 μL). (b): Conditions: the same as those used in Fig. 2a, except 25 %, w/v, ZnSO4 solution was utilized.
Optimization of eluent type. Conditions: as similar as those employed in Fig. 2b, except 35 mg of the sorbent was utilized and the adsorption time was 5 min.
Figures of merit of the suggested approach for the studied AFs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e)
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation for intra– and inter–day precisions at a concentration of 25 ng L − 1 of each analyte.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of assays to check the raw cow's milk samples matrix effect. Data are mean relative recovery ± standard deviation obtained from three repeated determination.

head.ce:title: MOF@N–doped carbon dots–based dispersive solid phase extraction combined dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction of several aflatoxins from raw cow’s milk samples prior to HPLC–FLD

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dispersive solid-phase extraction method based on metal–organic framework and nitrogen–doped carbon dot nanocomposite combined with magnetic ionic liquid-based dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction was established for the extraction of aflatoxins from milk samples. For this purpose, the proteins in the sample were precipitated using a zinc sulfate solution. After vortexing (for 5 min) and centrifuging, 35 mg of the sorbent were added into the aqueous phase obtained from previous step, and the mixture was vortexed for five minutes. In the following, the sorbent particles were separated and eluted with methanol in the presence of vortex agitation (4 min). After centrifuging, the eluent phase was mixed with magnetic ionic liquid (1- octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrachloroferrate) and rapidly injected into deionized water to perform the dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction procedure. Finally, the magnetic solvent was separated in the presence of a magnet and injected into a high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence detector after diluting with the mobile phase. Referring to the results, the limits of detection and quantification of the studied aflatoxins were in the ranges of 0.69–1.09 and 2.31–3.63 ng/L, respectively. Extraction recoveries were in the range of 61 to 73 %. At the end, the suggested approach was employed in the analysis of the aflatoxins in the raw cow’s milk samples collected from different regions of East Azarbaijan and AFM1 was found in several samples (34 out of 60 samples). Also, AFB1 was found in three samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Regarding high consumption of milk all over the world (owing to the fact that it contains nearly all essential supplements required for human wellbeing), it must be free of any pollutant [1–3]. The presence of AFs in milk samples, has evoked global concern for foodsafety owing to their negative effects on human health (carcinogenic toxicity, immunotoxicity, and mutagenicity) [4]. These toxic pollutants appear in foodstuffs due to the fungal growth of Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus species especially in warm and moist environments [5,6]. Up to now, more than twenty AFs have been identified. Among the known AFs, AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, AFG2, and AFM1 (known as the major derivative of AFB1 and produced through the hepatic biotransformation of ingested AFB1 by hydroxylation mechanisms) are the most abundant AFs found in foodstuffs [7,8]. These pollutants are relatively stable during pasteurization, sterilization, and storage of milk samples. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a fluorescence detector (FLD) is preferred to determine the AFs due to its high sensitivity and fairly good selectivity [9,10].As AFs concentration is usually low, they must be concentrated using a sample pretreatment method prior to chromatographic analysis [11]. The application of solid phase extraction (SPE) can be an interesting method for this purpose [12]. For the extraction of AFs and removing matrix interferences, mostly immunoaffinity columns have been utilized in SPE [13]. Nevertheless, these columns are expensive, and there is a possibility of column clogging. To solve these problems, a modified version of SPE nameddispersive solid phase extraction (DSPE) was introduced [14]. In DSPE, the sorbent particles are directly added to the sample, which can lead to large contact area between the analyte and sorbent in comparison with usual SPE and high extraction efficiency [15–21].

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered potential adsorbents in DSPE owing to their high surface area and porous structure [22–24]. Nevertheless, dispersibility of some MOFs like zeolitic imidazolate framework in aqueous solution is not a desirable point in DSPE based methods [25]. In order to solve this problem and also increase the selectivity of sorbent, this kind of MOF is modified with compounds like carbon dots (CDs) that possess polar groups in their structure and have small sizes and a high surface area [26]. Furthermore, the DSPE process is followed by a dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction (DLLME) to overcome the difficulties that appeared due tothe high volume of elution solvent (the possibility of obtaining low enrichment factors and consequently low limits of detection (LODs)) [27,28]. Ease of operation, rapidity, and effectiveness are the main merits of DLLME [29]. In recent years, magnetic ionic liquids (MILs) have been suggested for use in microextraction methods owing to their environmental friendliness and high magnetic properties [30].

In the present work, a MOF@nitrogen (N)-doped CD nanocomposite-based DSPE method in combination with MIL–based DLLME was developed for the extraction of AFs from raw cow's milk samples prior to their determination with HPLC–FLD. For this purpose, ZIF-67 was prepared and utilized in the composition of nanocomposite. It exhibits brilliant chemical and thermal stability because of its microporous structure and large surface area with adjustable pore size. CDs are carbon-based compounds with small size and high surface-to-volume ratio. The presence of polar groups CDs structure enhances their tendency towards polar compounds. Combination of CDs with other materials like ZIF-67, improves their physical properties and interactions to adsorb polar and semi-polar compound and their stability and dispersion in sample solution. In this method the following DLLME approach was done to more enrichment of the analytes and obtaining sensitive detection limit. On the other hand, the use of DLLME improve the method selectivity by eliminating of the adsorbed semi-polar or polar compounds onto the sorbent during DSPE step. The use of MIL facilitates the extraction procedure by removing the need for centrifugation. On other hand there was no need for vaporization of the solvent for injection into the analysis system. As far as we know, this is the first research on the employment of the suggested approach for the extraction of AFs from raw cow's milk samples.

AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, AFG2, and AFM1 analytical standards were bought from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MA, USA). 1–Hexyl–3–methylimidazolium tetrachloroferrate ([C6MIM] [FeCl4]) (≥97 %) and 1- octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrachloroferrate ([C8MIM] [FeCl4]) (≥97 %) MILs were bought from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MA, USA). HPLC-grade acetonitrile (ACN) and methanol, and analytical-reagent grade acetic acid, NaCl, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), 2-methylimidazole, chitosan, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, ethanol, acetone, and iso-propanol were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The analytes mixture stock solution was prepared in ACN (10 mg L − 1 of each).

Sixty raw milk samples were bought from local vendors of East Azarbaijan Province (Tabriz, Sarab, and Shabestar cities). Twenty samples were collected from each city. A 500 mL of each sample was collected and they were stored in glass bottles. One milk sample that was free of the studied AFs was selected and used as a blank. The absence of the analytes in blank milk was confirmed by a previously published method [32]. The samples were kept in a refrigerator (4 °C).

Herein, an Agilent HPLC (Model 1200) equipped with a FLD was used to quantify AFs. These analytes were separated by a Gemini C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm particle size) (Phenomenex, Torrance, CA, USA) held at 35 °C. For separation of the AFs, a gradient elution using water containing 1 % acetic acid: ACN: methanol mixture was employed at flow rate of 0.75 mL min–1 with the following program: water: ACN: methanol (70:15:15) for 20 min; change to water: ACN: methanol: (50:30:20) in 3 min; kept for 12 min; return to the primary conditions in 1 min; and kept for 5 min. 360 and 430 nm were set as the excited and emitted wavelengths for determination of the analytes. All injections were done into a 25 µL loop.
The sorbent preparation was done according a literature [25] and its steps are illustrated in supplementary data in detail.

First, 5 mL blank raw cow milk spiked with AFs (500 ng L − 1) or un–spiked sample was transferred into a 10–mL glass test tube. After that, 1.25 mL of zinc sulfate solution (25 %, w/v) was added to precipitate the sample proteins and the mixture was vortexed for 5 min. The liquid phase was taken into another tube after centrifuging (at 5000 rpm for 5 min) and mixed with 35 mg of sorbent to adsorb the analytes onto the sorbent. After vortexing mixture for 5 min (to enhance the contact area of sorbent with the solution) and centrifuging (at 5000 rpm for 5 min), the liquid phase was removed. In the following, 1.0 mL methanol was added into the sorbent to eluate the analytes under vortexing for 4 min. After centrifuging, the liquid phase containing the analytes was taken and dispersed into 5 mL deionized water after mixing with 60 μL [C8MIM] [FeCl4] MIL for more concentration. Consequently, the analytes extracted into the produced fine droplets of MIL that were collected in the presence of the magnet. This phase was taken and, after diluting with the mobile phase (till 25 μL), was injected into the separation system. The method steps are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The protein content of milk samples can hinder the extraction of analytes from the sample matrix. Therefore, precipitating their protein is the first step in the extraction of analytes from these samples. In the current research, ZnSO4 solution was employed to precipitate the proteins in the sample. As a result, the concentration and volume of the employed ZnSO4 solution can change the efficiency of the precipitating step. To select the optimum concentration, the utilized ZnSO4 solution concentration was varied in the range of 15 to 30 %, w/v. As shown in Fig. 2 a, 25 %, w/v is the optimum concentration (by using higher concentrations, the obtained ERs are not changed significantly, and at concentrations lower than this value, the precipitating step is not complete owing to lower extraction recovery (ERs)). So, the subsequent steps were done using a 25 %, w/v, ZnSO4 solution. The efficiency of the precipitating agent and consequently the whole extraction process can be affected by varying the ZnSO4 solution in the range of 0.75 to 1.75 mL. Referring to the outcomes in Fig. 1b, the ERs improved up to 1.25 mL and after that remained almost unchanged. Thus, 1.25 mL ZnSO4 solution was used in the next studies.

The vortexing time in the precipitating step should be optimized since it can affect the contact area between proteins and the precipitating agent, thereby affecting the efficiency of the precipitating process. This time was changed from 3 to 7 min. Based on the outcomes, the best results for analytes are seen at 5 min. Therefore, the mixture of ZnSO4 solution and sample solution was vortexed for 5 min in the subsequent steps.

Sorbent amount is the vital factor that can influence the capability of the DSPE procedure by providing different numbers of active adsorption sites that can adsorb the analytes. To optimize this parameter, different experiments were performed by employing the synthesized sorbent in the range of 25–45 mg. As can be seen in Fig. S1, the ERs of analytes improve up to 35 mg and then relatively decrease after that. The increase in ERs by enhancing the sorbent amount (up to 35 mg) was related to animprovement in the probability of adsorbing as a consequence of increasing adsorption sites. In addition, lower ERs at the amounts > 35 mg, were related to the fact that the eluent volume was not sufficient for the effective elution of the analytes from this amount of sorbent. Consequently, the following studies were done using 35 mg sorbent.

To obtain the best extraction conditions, the time of vortexing the mixture of sorbent and sample solution, which was named “adsorption time,” was also optimized. In this step, the vortexing time was varied in the range of 3 to 7 min. Referring to the results, 5 min is sufficient for the effective adsorption of the analytes, and we opted for the further experiments.

For selecting the suitable eluent that was also utilized as the dispersive solvent in the following DLLME step, different solvents, including acetone, iso-propanol, methanol, and ACN, were tested. Considering the results in Fig. 3 , the highest ERs are seen by using methanol as the elution solvent. This point can be attributed to the high elution capability of methanol and its ability to disperse the MIL as tiny droplets in the DLLME step. Thus, the following experiments were done using methanol as an elution/dispersive solvent.

The volume of elution/dispersive solvent that could affect both DSPE and DLLME steps was also varied in the range of 0.5 to 1.5 mL. The outcomes illustrate that, ERs of analytes improve until 1 mL and decrease after that. Based on the outcomes in Fig. S2, low volumes of methanol (less than 1 mL) are not enough for the efficient elution of the analytes from the sorbent surface. This is because at higher volumes, the solubility of analytes increased, which led to lower ERs. Thus, 1 mL ofmethanol was used in the subsequent studies.

In the desorption step, the effect of the vortexing time of the mixture of sorbent particles and elution sorbent was also investigated. To do so, the mixture of sorbent and eluent was vortexed for 3 to 7 min. Regarding the results, the ERs of analytes improved until 4 min and then remained almost unchanged.

To establish an effective DLLME procedure, selecting a suitable extraction solvent is of great importance. In the present work, to guarantee the greenness of the proposed work, MILs were employed as extraction solvents. For this parameter, [C6MIM] [FeCl4] and [C8MIM] [FeCl4] MILs were tested as extractant. According to results in Fig. S3, [C8MIM] [FeCl4] MIL provided the highest ERs and opted for the next experiments.

The volume of utilized extractant in the DLLME process that can affect the inappropriate formation of cloudy solutions and the final sedimented phase was also investigated. This solvent should be used at a volume as low as possible. To optimize these parameters, various volumes (45 to 70 μL) of [C8MIM] [FeCl4] MIL were used. Based on the experimental observations, by increasing the MIL volume to 60 μL, ERs of analytes are increased, and after that, they remain almost constant. Consequently, 60 µL were used in the following experiments.

Linear range (LR), LOD, limit of quantification (LOQ), coefficient of determination (r2), ER, and repeatability as analytical parameters of the present work were calculated under the optimum conditions and summarized in Table 1 . The method LOD (signal (S)/noise (N) = 3) and LOQ (S/N = 10) were obtained in the ranges of 0.69–1.09 and 2.31–3.63 ng/L, respectively. Linearity of the suggested method obtained from the calibration curves were excellent (r2 ≥ 0.994) for the wide LRs. Intra– (n = 6) and inter–day (n = 4) RSD values for the AFs at the concentration of 25 ng L − 1 were ≤ 5.3 and 6.2 %, respectively. ER was also defined as total amount of the analyte (no) that was extracted into the final phase (nfin) and calculated according to the following formula:
ER \% =
n
fin
n 0
× 100 =
C
fin
×
V
fin
C 0
×
V
aq
× 100
Where Vfin and Vaq indicate volumes of the final phase and aqueous phase, respectively. The ERs for analytes were in the range of 61–73 % according to the outcomes.

The study aimed to investigate the effectiveness of the suggested approach for analyzing AFs in raw cow's milk samples. To reach this aim, sixty raw milk samples were analyzed using the introduced method under final conditions in triplicate. According to the data, AFM1 was found in thirty-four samples. The AFMs concentrations were 32–223 ng L − 1 in ten samples collected from Tabriz, 126–331 ng L − 1 in eight samples collected from Sarab, and 42–239 ng L − 1 in ten samples collected from Shabestar. In three samples collected from Sarab, AFB1 was also determined in 2.1, 4.2, and 6.3 ng/L. Therefore, to keep humans against the negative effects of AFs exposure, their determination in milk samples and comparison with the maximum residue limits regulated by the international organizations are important (according to the US FDA, the concentration of AFM1 and the total AFs (B1, B2, G1 and G2) should not exceed 0.5 and 15 ng g−1, respectively). It must be noted that AFM1 and AFB1 levels are related to the diet source of lactating animals, management policy, and ecological factors. The absence of aflatoxins in the milks can be related to feeding of the animals with fresh forage due to the fact that concentrate and silage can be polluted with mycotoxins under improper storage condition [31]. The analytes concentrations were listed in Table S1 in detail. In the next step, five raw cow's milk samples were randomly chosen and, along with a blank sample, spiked with AFs at three concentration levels (10, 25, and 100 ng L − 1) to investigate the matrix effect. The outcomes revealed as mean relative recoveries in Table 2 suggest that the suggested method is appropriate for analyzing AFs in milk samples, as the sample matrix effect is negligible (the obtained relative recoveries that were calculated from dividing the analyte peak areas in the spiked real sample by the analyte peak area in the spiked blank sample and multiplying the ratio by 100, were in the range of 87 to 103 %).

The efficiency of the suggested method was evaluated by comparing its LOD, LR, RSD, and extraction time with those of previously reported methods. Referring to the data in Table 3 , LODs obtained with the present work are lower than those obtained with the other approaches except the methods done based on IL-DLLME [37], solid phase microextraction (SPME) [38], and DSPE-DLLME approach using deep eutectic solvent (DES) [32]. In spite of sensitive LODs of SPME and IL-DLLME methods, they were time-consuming. Although the obtained LOD of this method is higher than the LOD of DSPE-DES-DLLME [32] for AFM1, but the introduced method can extract five analytes simultaneously whereas only two mycotoxins can be determined by the DSPE-DES-DLLME approach. Furthermore, more amounts of adsorbent are needed for extraction of two analytes by the established method. The present work indicates wider LRs than the other approaches. RSDs% of the suggested method are better than or comparable with those of other ones. In addition, the offered method can be completed in a shorter extraction time, which indicates the rapidity of the method.

In the current research, a DSPE–DLLME method in combination with HPLC–FLD was employed for the extraction and determination of AFs. This method can overcome the difficulties of using individual DSPE and DLLME. Herein, the porous structure of MOF and the high adsorption capacity of N–doped CD offered high ERs for the studied AFs. As well, the use of MIL as extractant in DLLME step makes the approach environmentally friendly (these solvents possess low toxicity in comparison with halogenated solvents) and rapid (due to the removal of the centrifuging step in the DLLME process). The proposed method yielded satisfactory results, such as low LODs and LOQs, high ERs, low RSDs, and wide LRs.

Ghadir Khalilipour: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Amir Reza Karamibonari: Supervision, Methodology. Mohamadhossein Movassaghghazani: Supervision, Methodology. Jalal Shayegh: Validation, Formal analysis. Mohammad Reza Afshar Mogaddam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present paper is the result of a dissertation of first author for the Ph.D. program in the animal feed hygiene field at Islamic Azad University, Shabestar Branch, Shabestar, Iran.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110806.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In–syringe dispersive micro–solid phase extraction method for the HPLC–fluorescence determination of aflatoxins in milk
Food Control

Pak. J. Anal. Environ.

J. Chromatogr. b.

Front. Microbiol.

J. Chromatogr. A

Chromatogr. A 1673 (2022) 463099.

J. Sep. Sci.
